1: shadow Be not silent, O God of my praise! Calling on God not to be silent in the face of accusation — the prayer Christ implicitly prays in his sufferings. The Father answers by raising him: The stone that the builders rejected has become the cornerstone; this is the Lord's doing, and it is marvelous in our eyes (Psalms 118:22-23; Matthew 21:42). The resurrection is God refusing to be silent about his Son.
2: shadow For wicked and deceitful mouths are opened against me, speaking against me with lying tongues. Wicked and deceitful mouths — the false witnesses at Jesus's trial: the chief priests and the whole council were seeking false testimony against Jesus that they might put him to death, but they found none, though many false witnesses came forward (Matthew 26:59-60). The lying tongues of v2 opened against innocent David are opened against innocent Christ in the Sanhedrin's midnight court.
3: shadow They encircle me with words of hate, and attack me without cause. Attacked without cause — John 15:25: they hated me without a cause. The encirclement with hate is the arrest in Gethsemane, the ring of soldiers, the mob crying crucify him! The causeless attack on the innocent becomes the ground of his atoning work: he who was hated without cause bore the hatred we deserved.
4: shadow In return for my love they accuse me, but I give myself to prayer. Love repaid with accusation, responded to with prayer — Christ at the cross: Father, forgive them, for they know not what they do (Luke 23:34). Love given and rejected; prayer offered for the accusers. Hebrews 7:25: he always lives to make intercession for them. Even now, Christ gives himself to prayer — interceding for those who once accused him.
5: shadow So they reward me evil for good, and hatred for my love. Evil for good and hatred for love — the moral inversion of the crucifixion. Christ gives every good thing and receives in return betrayal, accusation, death. He came to his own, and his own people did not receive him (John 1:11). The reward of hatred for love becomes the occasion for the greatest act of love: giving his life for those who hated him (Romans 5:10).
6: shadow Appoint a wicked man against him; let an accuser stand at his right hand. Accuser at the right hand — the courtroom imagery reversed in Christ. The accuser (Satan = the accuser; Revelation 12:10) who stands against humanity is defeated at the cross. Christ stands as our advocate: if anyone does sin, we have an advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous (1 John 2:1). He has taken the accusation so that the accuser has nothing left to say against those in him (Romans 8:33).
7: shadow When he is tried, let him come forth guilty; let his prayer be counted as sin! Prayer counted as sin — the grotesque judgment the court rendered on Christ: his claims were treated as blasphemy deserving death (Matthew 26:65). The innocent one was found guilty. But God reversed the verdict: the resurrection is the Father's judgment that Christ is righteous (1 Timothy 3:16: vindicated by the Spirit).
8: direct May his days be few; may another take his office! Acts 1:20: Peter explicitly quotes this verse when explaining the need to replace Judas: His office let another take. The betrayer of the innocent — Judas, who handed over the suffering servant — fulfills the imprecation of v8. The selection of Matthias (Acts 1:26) is the direct fulfillment of "let another take his office" — the apostolic vacancy created by betrayal is filled by divine appointment.
9: shadow May his children be fatherless and his wife a widow! The consequences falling on the betrayer's household — historically fulfilled in Judas's death (Acts 1:18) and the desolation of his household. Judas's judgment is not a contradiction of the gospel but its confirmation: the one who hands over the holy Son of God receives the death he facilitated.
10: shadow May his children wander about and beg, seeking food far from the ruins they inhabit! Children wandering and begging — the consequences of betrayal. The contrast is with Christ's promise: I will not leave you as orphans; I will come to you (John 14:18). Those who betray Christ are left fatherless; those who follow him receive the Spirit of adoption crying Abba, Father! (Romans 8:15).
11: shadow May the creditor seize all that he has; may strangers plunder the fruits of his toil! Creditors seizing everything — Judas received thirty pieces of silver (Matthew 26:15), threw them into the temple (Matthew 27:5), and the money was used to buy the potter's field (Matthew 27:7). The wages of betrayal became property of strangers — providential irony: the betrayer's payment purchased the field of his burial.
12: shadow Let there be none to extend kindness to him, nor anyone to pity his fatherless children! No kindness extended — the stark end of one who showed no mercy in betraying the innocent. The contrast with Christ: even on the cross he shows kindness to the thief beside him (today you will be with me in paradise, Luke 23:43) and provides for his mother (John 19:26-27). The absence of mercy toward the betrayer makes Christ's mercy shine more brightly.
13: shadow May his posterity be cut off; may his name be blotted out in the second generation! Name blotted out — the anti-Deuteronomic judgment: rather than a name remembered for generations (Proverbs 10:7), the betrayer's name is cut off. Revelation 3:5: Christ promises to those who overcome that I will never blot his name out of the book of life. Those faithful to Christ have their names written; those who betray him face erasure.
14: shadow May the iniquity of his fathers be remembered before the LORD, and let not the sin of his mother be blotted out. Ancestral iniquity remembered — the gravity of covenant betrayal reaching back through generations. The cross settles all such ancestral accounts for those who are in Christ: canceling the record of debt that stood against us with its legal demands. This he set aside, nailing it to the cross (Colossians 2:13-14). The betrayer's debt is left uncanceled; the believer's is nailed to the cross.
15: shadow Let them be before the LORD continually, that he may cut off the memory of them from the earth! Memory cut off — the permanent erasure of the unfaithful. In contrast, Christ is the one whose memory is perpetually proclaimed: do this in remembrance of me (1 Corinthians 11:24-25). The church's Eucharistic remembrance keeps alive the name and work of the one the betrayer tried to silence.
16: shadow For he did not remember to show kindness, but pursued the poor and needy and the brokenhearted, to put them to death. Not showing kindness — the indictment of those who oppose the poor and brokenhearted. Christ identifies himself with them: the Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because he has anointed me to proclaim good news to the poor... to bind up the brokenhearted (Luke 4:18; Isaiah 61:1). To pursue the poor and brokenhearted is to pursue Christ (Matthew 25:45).
17: shadow He loved to curse; let curses come upon him! He did not delight in blessing; may it be far from him! The one who loved cursing receiving curses — Galatians 6:7: God is not mocked, for whatever one sows, that will he also reap. But Christ voluntarily absorbed the curse that falls on the guilty: Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us (Galatians 3:13). The curse earned by treachery was borne by the innocent — so that those in him receive blessing instead.
18: shadow He clothed himself with cursing as his coat; may it soak into his body like water, like oil into his bones! Cursing soaking in like oil — the perversion of the priestly anointing (Psalms 133:2). Rather than sacred oil of blessing, it is the oil of self-inflicted curse. The contrast with Christ the Anointed (Messiah) who became a curse to break the curse (Galatians 3:13) — the true anointing brings blessing where treachery brought cursing.
19: shadow May it be like a garment that he wraps around him, like a belt that he puts on every day! Cursing worn daily like a garment — the inescapable consequence of betraying the innocent. The garment imagery recalls Christ's garments divided at the cross (John 19:23-24), and the robe of righteousness he gives to those who follow him (Revelation 19:8). The betrayer wraps himself in cursing; the believer is clothed with Christ (Galatians 3:27).
20: shadow May this be the reward of my accusers from the LORD, of those who speak evil against my life! The reward of accusers — the eschatological judgment. Revelation 20:12-15: the books are opened; those whose names are not in the book of life face the lake of fire. But Christ prays for his accusers (Luke 23:34) — his intercession delays and in many cases transforms the reward, converting accusers into worshipers.
21: shadow But you, O GOD my Lord, deal on my behalf for your name's sake; because your steadfast love is good, deliver me! Calling on God's name and steadfast love for deliverance — the prayer Christ prays in Gethsemane: Father, if you are willing, remove this cup from me. Nevertheless, not my will, but yours, be done (Luke 22:42). The appeal to God's steadfast love is the appeal of the innocent sufferer who trusts the Father through the darkest hour.
22: shadow For I am poor and needy, and my heart is stricken within me. Poor, needy, heart stricken — Christ's Gethsemane condition: my soul is very sorrowful, even to death (Matthew 26:38). He who was rich became poor (2 Corinthians 8:9); he who was Lord of all entered the depths of human need and anguish. He entered this poverty voluntarily so that through his poverty we might become rich (2 Corinthians 8:9).
23: shadow I am gone like a shadow at evening; I am shaken off like a locust. Like a fading shadow — the mortal vulnerability of the suffering one. Christ's mortality was real: he was genuinely subject to death (Hebrews 2:14 — he himself likewise partook of flesh and blood... that through death he might destroy the one who has the power of death). He submitted to the evening shadow of mortality so that death could not hold him and those in him escape its power.
24: shadow My knees are weak through fasting; my body has become gaunt, with no fat. Weakness through fasting — Christ's forty-day fast in the wilderness (Matthew 4:2: afterward he was hungry). He entered genuine physical weakness — not docetic strength disguised as weakness but actual bodily diminishment. In that weakened state he refused to turn stones to bread, trusting God's provision over physical need (Matthew 4:4).
25: direct I am an object of scorn to my accusers; when they see me, they wag their heads. Matthew 27:39 fulfills this directly: those who passed by derided him, wagging their heads and saying, You who would destroy the temple and rebuild it in three days, save yourself! The very gesture of contempt — the head-wag of the scornful — is specifically enacted at the crucifixion, marking the scene as fulfillment of the suffering servant's experience in Ps 109.
26: shadow Help me, O LORD my God! Save me according to your steadfast love! The cry for salvation by steadfast love — answered in the resurrection. He was heard because of his reverence (Hebrews 5:7). The Father's steadfast love answers Christ's prayer by raising him from the dead; the resurrection is God's yes to the prayer of his Son. Those who cry the same prayer are heard in Christ's name.
27: shadow Let them know that this is your hand; you, O LORD, have done it! God's hand doing it — the disciples recognizing the resurrection as God's act. Acts 2:24: God raised him up, loosing the pangs of death; Acts 2:36: let all the house of Israel therefore know for certain that God has made him both Lord and Christ, this Jesus whom you crucified. The knowledge that this is God's hand is the content of the Pentecost proclamation.
28: shadow They may curse, but you will bless; when they arise, they will be put to shame, but your servant will be glad! They curse, God blesses — the resurrection reversal. The accusers of the cross thought they had ended the story with a curse; God blessed by raising the crucified one. He is not here; he has risen (Matthew 28:6). The servants of the crucified Lord are glad because the one they serve is vindicated — and their gladness is permanent: no one will take your joy from you (John 16:22).
29: shadow May my accusers be clothed with dishonor; may they be wrapped in their own shame as in a cloak! Accusers clothed with dishonor — the eschatological reversal of the cross. The soldiers clothed Jesus in a purple robe of mockery (John 19:2-3). At his return, the mocked becomes the glorified: behold, he is coming with the clouds, and every eye will see him, even those who pierced him (Revelation 1:7). The mockers face the one they clothed in shame.
30: shadow With my mouth I will give great thanks to the LORD; I will praise him in the midst of the throng. Praising in the midst of the throng — Hebrews 2:12 applies Psalms 22:22 to Christ: in the midst of the congregation I will sing your praise. The risen Christ praises the Father in the midst of the church. The thanksgiving of v30 is Christ's post-resurrection praise in the assembly of his disciples, in which all believers are caught up.
31: shadow For he stands at the right hand of the needy one, to save him from those who condemn his soul to death. Standing at the right hand of the needy — the intercession of Christ. Romans 8:34: Christ Jesus is the one who died — more than that, who was raised — who is at the right hand of God, who indeed is interceding for us. The one who was himself condemned now stands as advocate for all who are condemned. The needy of Ps 109 find Christ at their right hand.
